text: The video shows the herniated contents protruding through the defect, with visible tissue surrounding the area.
The surgeon begins preperitoneal dissection starting at the falciform ligament, carefully separating the peritoneal layers to expose underlying structures.
The surgeon gains access in the upper abdomen and strategically places the surgical ports for the procedure.
The surgeon performs dissection and begins peritoneal closure, which appears challenging due to the proximity of the ports to the surgical site.
The surgeon performs a standard pre-peritoneal dissection, reaching the area of the hernias where adipose tissue is visible.
The scrub nurse applies manual pressure at the bedside to assist in the reduction of hernia contents during the procedure.
The surgical field reveals multiple defects and hernias, which the surgeon will address sequentially.
The surgeon achieves a satisfying reduction of a large fat-containing hernia, visibly compressing and repositioning the herniated tissue back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon continues along the midline, visually identifying multiple hernia defects in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon aggressively reduces the herniated fat, which is easily accomplished due to the lack of adhesions.
The surgeon exposes the xiphoid process and completes the dissection and reduction of the incarcerated hernia contents, revealing the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon begins developing a healthy space in the abdominal cavity to accommodate the placement of surgical mesh.
The surgeon carefully checks for any nearby viscera before proceeding with subdiaphragmatic dissection, ensuring a clear surgical field.
The central tendon of the diaphragm is coming into view as the surgeon aims to identify it during the procedure involving an M-I hernia.
The surgeon uses cautery to control small bleeders in the surgical field, applying the device carefully to manage bleeding.
The surgeon continues to identify the central tendon of the diaphragm, which is visible in the surgical field.
The final view shows approximately seven centimeters of space from the superior aspect of the defect, prepared for mesh coverage.
The surgeon encounters difficulty in re-approximating the fascia during the repair of a subxiphoid hernia, highlighting the challenge of aligning the tissue edges.
The surgeon strategically closes the smaller defect transversely, ensuring precise alignment of the tissue edges.
The surgeon addresses the upper defect by performing a vertical closure, aligning the tissue edges vertically.
The surgeon successfully approximates the tissue, achieving a smooth closure without any visible complications.
The surgeon places a heavyweight mesh measuring fifteen by eighteen centimeters to achieve circumferential coverage around small hernia defects.
The surgeon completes fixation of the mesh with absorbable sutures and prepares for the closure of the peritoneum.
The surgeon performs a straightforward peritoneal closure, with the camera positioned close to the surgical field, requiring careful maneuvering to maintain a clear view of the action.
